summary: The author examines when ZooKeeper is appropriate versus overkill for distributed systems, framing it as a fundamental question architects must answer. She observes a clear divide: platforms like Hadoop, Storm, and Kafka use ZooKeeper for coordination, while distributed databases like MongoDB and Cassandra implement their own logic. Drawing an analogy to how RDBMSs optimize by bypassing OS functionality, she argues that databases have competitive incentives to own their coordination logic. However, for most systems, ZooKeeper represents a necessary 'operating system' component for distributed computing, since building correct distributed coordination from scratch requires expertise most teams lack.
key_points: Distributed systems split into two camps: those using ZooKeeper as a service (Hadoop, Storm, Kafka) and those implementing internal coordination (MongoDB, Riak, Cassandra)
Distributed databases have competitive incentives to own coordination logic—simpler deployment and tuned performance for their specific use cases
Successful distributed coordination requires more than a Paxos library; it demands upfront HA design, proper quorum deployment, and understanding how GC and other system behaviors affect coordination
Even Google chose to build Chubby as a service rather than a client library because most developers don't naturally think in distributed coordination terms
For most systems outside specialized databases, implementing custom coordination makes as little sense as writing a custom filesystem for a web app
money_quotes: The answer that I prefer to give is almost always 'yes, but keep these things in mind before you roll it out for this purpose.'
ZooKeeper, or a service like it, is a necessary core component of the 'operating system' for distributed computing.
It does not make sense for most systems to implement their own distributed coordination, any more than it makes sense to implement your own file system to run your RESTful web app.
Installing and administering one type of thing (the database itself) is by definition simpler than installing and administering 2 things.
Even with such a library, you would need to design your application up-front to think about high availability.
It makes sense to prefer to burn your own developer dollars to engineer around the edge cases, so as to make a simpler product for your customers.
key_insight: ZooKeeper should be viewed as a fundamental operating system service for distributed computing—most teams should use it rather than reinvent coordination logic, but specialized databases have legitimate reasons to build their own.
